name: Sacsayhuaman Walls
description: The Sacsayhuaman Walls, located near Cusco, Peru, are a massive complex of precisely fitted stone walls. Mainstream archaeology attributes their construction to the Inca civilization, primarily during the 15th century, viewing them as a military fortress, ceremonial center, or a combination of both. The intricate interlocking of stones, some weighing over 100 tons, is considered a testament to Inca engineering prowess and social organization. However, alternative theories propose that the walls are far older and built by a pre-Inca civilization with advanced, possibly lost, technologies. Proponents of these theories often point to the size and precision of the stones, arguing that Inca technology was insufficient to move and shape them with such accuracy. They suggest the stones might have been softened or molded using unknown techniques. Some even propose extraterrestrial involvement or the existence of a lost civilization possessing advanced knowledge. Mainstream archaeology refutes these claims, citing evidence of Inca quarrying techniques, ramp systems, and the use of levers and manpower to move the stones. They acknowledge the impressive scale of the project but maintain that it falls within the capabilities of Inca engineering, achieved through decades of labor and sophisticated planning. The precise fitting of the stones is attributed to meticulous shaping and placement, not advanced technology beyond the Inca's known abilities. The debate centers around the interpretation of available evidence and the plausibility of alternative explanations in the absence of supporting archaeological data for advanced pre-Inca civilizations or technologies.
subtitle: Ancient Inca Walls and Alternative Theories

claims.claim: The Sacsayhuaman stones were moved and shaped using advanced, lost technologies.
claims.evidence: The sheer size and precise fitting of the stones are beyond the capabilities of known Inca technology.
claims.criticism: Inca quarrying techniques, ramp systems, and manpower could have moved and shaped the stones over many years.

claims.claim: The walls are far older than mainstream archaeology suggests, predating the Inca civilization.
claims.evidence: Some stones show signs of weathering or erosion that suggest a much greater age than attributed to the Inca.
claims.criticism: Weathering can be caused by various factors, and dating stone structures is complex. No conclusive evidence supports pre-Inca construction.

claims.claim: The stones were softened or molded, rather than cut and shaped, using an unknown process.
claims.evidence: The irregular shapes and tight interlocking of the stones suggest a fluidity not achievable through traditional stone cutting.
claims.criticism: The stones were meticulously shaped and fitted together through a subtractive process of carving and polishing, as evidenced by tool marks and unfinished stones at nearby quarries.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Promoter of alternative theories regarding ancient civilizations and advanced technologies.

keyFigures.name: Brien Foerster
keyFigures.role: Researcher and author who promotes alternative theories about the Sacsayhuaman Walls.

keyFigures.name: Jean-Pierre Protzen
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who studied Inca quarrying and construction techniques.

mythologyConnections: Stories of giants building ancient structures

timeline.event: Mainstream archaeological dating places the primary construction of Sacsayhuaman.
Spanish conquest and subsequent damage to the Sacsayhuaman complex.
Rise of alternative theories regarding the age and construction of Sacsayhuaman.

sources.author: Foerster, Brien
sources.work: Lost Ancient Technology of Peru and Bolivia
sources.title: Foerster, Brien
